title: podman should expose OOM killer activity on machine VM
body:
I spent a few hours today trying to debug a container that was just restarting endlessly. `podman logs`, showing the container's stdout/stderr, did not give any clue, so I went through a winding process of modifying the container image to install a debugger, adding `SYS_PTRACE`, etc. etc.

It turned out it was just getting killed by the OOM killer in the podman machine. It was pretty far down my triage list to check `podman machine ssh sudo dmesg` etc.

Since by default the machine only gets 2 GB, this seems like a vexing situation that users could find themselves in once or twice.

### Suggest potential solution

It would be useful if the podman CLI could more clearly expose if there has been OOM activity, such as noting the restart reason in `podman ps` or something.

comments.body:
Hi @rgov 

I just wanted to let you know we do expose when a container is killed due to OOM:
```
podman inspect oomtest --format {{.State.OOMKilled}}
```

I went into more detail here: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25990

Now we have two reports of users not easily discovering this information, it may be something we want to bubble up.

Off the top of my head, when we run `podman ps` the information displayed is dynamic using a template. If a container is killed due to OOM Killer, we could then enabled that extra column in the output.

Or we could add the details to the status like `Exited (0) 7 days ago (OOM)`

Do you have any other suggestions?

That's nice to know! I was inspecting my container's status through `docker compose ps` which uses Podman's `docker.sock` but the columns displayed are out of Podman's control. I was only seeing the "Up for *x* seconds" status and not the exit status before the restart.

Is it permissible to inject into the `podman logs` output a message like `Exited (9) (OOM)`, or does this output need to be untouched by the container engine?

Otherwise, I am not sure if what side channel exists for communicating with the user.

If the container gets automatically restarted then I don't see how we can preserve that information in inspect/ps output. Once the container is started again that information is "flushed" away basically as the state gets reset.

>Is it permissible to inject into the podman logs output a message like Exited (9) (OOM), or does this output need to be untouched by the container engine?

The logs are expected to be 1 to 1 what the container wrote so I don't think we should ever inject anything there.


The only way I could think of is using podman events. There is the `died` event so I think we could attach the oom killed field as attribute there.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Hello all,

I know I already assigned myself to this issue, but I wanted to briefly introduce myself. I'm a computer science student at the University of Texas at Austin, and I'm hoping to work on this issue as part of a final project for a Virtualization course.

Please correct me if I'm wrong, but based on what's already been discussed, it looks like the podman events approach seems like the preferred way of fixing this. Is this still the case, or is there now a better way of doing this? I'd appreciate any pointers on which files to look at and any documentation to look through.

My project timeline runs for the next few weeks, so I'll be actively working on this during that period.

Thanks in advance, and I'm looking forward to contributing!
